event: Susanna accosted by the two elders
symbols: Susanna's exposed vulnerability — innocence assailed by corrupt authority
two elders pressing close — the abuse of judicial power for selfish desire
red drapery — passion, danger, and the blood of unjust accusation
garden setting — the private space violated by predatory men
statue in background — the silent witness of stone contrasted with living injustice
description: A dramatic painting showing a young woman (Susanna) seated, partially covered by a rich red drapery, her upper body exposed. She turns away in distress as two elderly bearded men press close behind her from the left, one reaching toward her shoulder. Her expression conveys alarm and revulsion. A small classical statue is visible in the upper right background. The painting uses strong contrasts between the luminous flesh of Susanna and the darker, shadowed forms of the elders.
theological_description: This scene captures the moment of crisis in the story of Susanna (Daniel 13 in the Septuagint/Vulgate), when two corrupt judges corner the virtuous wife in her garden and demand she yield to their lust or face false charges of adultery. Susanna's anguished choice — 'I am completely trapped. If I do this, it is death for me; if I do not, I cannot escape your hands. I choose not to do it; I will fall into your hands, rather than sin in the sight of the Lord' — is one of the most powerful declarations of moral courage in the biblical tradition. Her refusal echoes Joseph's rejection of Potiphar's wife: 'How then could I do such a wicked thing and sin against God?' (Genesis 39:9). Susanna's willingness to face death rather than sin embodies the principle that 'we must obey God rather than human beings' (Acts 5:29). God vindicates her through the young Daniel's wisdom, exposing the elders' lies and delivering the innocent. The story powerfully illustrates Psalm 37:28: 'The LORD loves the just and will not forsake his faithful ones.'

scripture_refs.reason: Joseph refusing to sin against God despite pressure — the same moral pattern as Susanna's refusal
The Lord not forsaking His faithful ones — Susanna's ultimate vindication by God
Obeying God rather than human beings — Susanna's choice to face death rather than sin
Vindicate me, Lord, for I have led a blameless life — Susanna's cry for divine justice
